lesson_title: External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.back: An identifier with external linkage can be seen and used from other translation units (files). Functions and non-const global variables have external linkage by default.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: To forward declare a global variable from another file, use the {{c1::extern}} keyword: {{c2::extern int g_value;}}
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: What's the difference between 'int g_x;' and 'extern int g_x;'?
cards.back: 'int g_x;' is a definition (creates the variable). 'extern int g_x;' is a forward declaration (tells the compiler the variable exists elsewhere).
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.question: What's wrong with this code?
cards.answer: This is a redefinition, not a forward declaration. Use 'extern int g_value;' in b.cpp instead.
cards.explanation: Without 'extern', this creates a new variable definition, causing a multiple definition error. Use 'extern' to forward declare a variable defined elsewhere.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: What is the default linkage for functions?
cards.back: External linkage. Functions are external by default, which is why you can call functions defined in other files.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: What is the default linkage for non-const global variables?
cards.back: External linkage. Non-const global variables have external linkage by default.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: To give a const global variable external linkage, use {{c1::extern const int g_value{ 5 };}} in the definition and {{c2::extern const int g_value;}} in the forward declaration.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.question: How do you make this const global variable accessible from other files?
cards.code:
// a.cpp
const int g_max{ 100 }; // currently internal linkage
cards.explanation: Const globals have internal linkage by default. Adding 'extern' gives them external linkage, making them accessible from other files (with a forward declaration).
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: Can an extern declaration include an initializer?
cards.back: Yes, but then it becomes a definition, not just a forward declaration. This defeats the purpose of using extern for forward declarations.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: Where should extern forward declarations for global variables be placed?
cards.back: In a header file that can be included wherever the variable is needed. This ensures consistency and avoids errors from manual duplication.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: A variable forward declaration using {{c1::extern}} tells the compiler about a variable {{c2::defined elsewhere}}, without creating a new variable.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.front: Do function forward declarations need the 'extern' keyword?
cards.back: No. Functions have external linkage by default, so 'extern' is optional and typically omitted from function forward declarations.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.concept: Pattern for sharing global variables across files
cards.explanation:
Best practice for sharing global variables:
  • Define the variable in one .cpp file with external linkage
  • Create a header file with an extern forward declaration
  • Include the header in any file that needs to use the variable
  • This ensures consistency and prevents errors
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: Functions have {{c1::external linkage}} by default, which is why you can call functions {{c2::defined in other files}}.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: A variable forward declaration uses {{c1::extern}} without an {{c2::initializer}}: extern int g_value;
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: To give a const global external linkage: {{c1::extern const}} int g_value{{c2::{ 5 };}} in the definition, and {{c3::extern const int g_value;}} in the forward declaration.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations

cards.text: Variables with {{c1::external linkage}} can be seen and used from {{c2::other translation units}}, while variables with internal linkage are limited to {{c3::a single file}}.
cards.source: 7.7 - External linkage and variable forward declarations
